rationale: All three ground-truth facts are present and correctly stated in the answer. The must-contain criterion (Amodei's late-January 2025 essay response: the ~$6M figure is misleading, DeepSeek did not 'do for $6M what cost US companies billions', and he used the episode to argue for stronger chip export controls) is satisfied. The answer explicitly mentions the essay title 'On DeepSeek and Export Controls' and the late January 2025 timing. It quotes Amodei rejecting the 'do for $6M what cost US companies billions' narrative. And it notes the essay argued for stronger export controls (implicit in the essay's purpose and explicit in the summary).

fact_verdicts.fact: Amodei's essay 'On DeepSeek and Export Controls' was published in late January 2025
fact_verdicts.evidence: In late January 2025, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei published an essay titled *"On DeepSeek and Export Controls"* on his personal site (darioamodei.com) and shared it on X (Twitter) on January 30, 2025.

fact_verdicts.fact: Amodei disclosed Claude 3.5 Sonnet cost 'a few tens of millions' to train, 9-12 months before DeepSeek-V3
fact_verdicts.evidence: He wrote: *"Claude 3.5 Sonnet is a mid-sized model that cost a few $10M's to train (I won't give an exact number)."* He noted that Sonnet's training was conducted 9–12 months prior, while DeepSeek's V3 model was trained in November/December 2024.

fact_verdicts.fact: He characterized DeepSeek's model as close to US models 7-10 months older, at lower but not revolutionary cost
fact_verdicts.evidence: *"I think a fair statement is 'DeepSeek produced a model close to the performance of US models 7-10 months older, for a good deal less cost (but not anywhere near the ratios people have suggested)'."*

rationale: All three ground-truth facts are present in the answer. The answer explicitly mentions February 2025, quotes 'DID NOT TOUCH THE DMA LAYER AT ALL', and quotes the rejection of 'as a DMA maintainer I control what the DMA code is used for'. The must-contain criterion about Torvalds's February 2025 rebuttal is satisfied. Therefore the verdict is correct.

fact_verdicts.fact: The dispute was over Rust bindings calling the kernel's DMA API, February 2025
fact_verdicts.evidence: In early 2025, Christoph Hellwig... objected to the merging of Rust abstractions for the DMA-mapping layer. ... Torvalds responded publicly ... on or around February 20, 2025.

fact_verdicts.fact: Torvalds wrote the pull request 'DID NOT TOUCH THE DMA LAYER AT ALL'
fact_verdicts.evidence: Torvalds stated: "the pull request you objected to DID NOT TOUCH THE DMA LAYER AT ALL. It was literally just another user of it, in a completely separate subdirectory, that didn't change the code you maintain in _any_ way, shape, or form."

fact_verdicts.fact: Torvalds rejected the position 'as a DMA maintainer I control what the DMA code is used for' as 'not how any of this works'
fact_verdicts.evidence: Torvalds rejected Hellwig's assertion of authority, writing: "what you have been doing is basically saying 'as a DMA maintainer I control what the DMA code is used for'." He characterized this as an overreach...

rationale: Facts 1–3 are present with exact quotes. rationale: All three ground-truth facts are present and correctly stated. The must-contain criterion (roughly 186,000 more employees) is satisfied with the exact numbers from the respective 10-Ks. The early-stop note does not affect correctness.

fact_verdicts.fact: Microsoft reported ~228,000 full-time employees as of June 30, 2025
fact_verdicts.evidence: Microsoft (FY2025, ended June 30, 2025): 228,000 employees. This figure is reported in Microsoft's 10-K for the fiscal year ended June 30, 2025 (filed July 30, 2025).

fact_verdicts.fact: Nvidia reported ~42,000 employees in its FY2026 10-K (fiscal year ended January 2026)

fact_verdicts.fact: The difference is approximately 186,000
fact_verdicts.evidence: 228,000 (Microsoft) − 42,000 (Nvidia) = 186,000 more employees at Microsoft.

rationale: All three ground-truth facts are present in the answer. Fact 1: score, date, stadium, and scorers are all stated. fact_verdicts.fact: Mexico 2-0 South Africa, June 11, 2026, Estadio Azteca; scorers Julián Quiñones (9') and Raúl Jiménez
fact_verdicts.evidence: The opening match of the 2026 FIFA World Cup was played on **Thursday, June 11, 2026**, at **Estadio Azteca** in Mexico City, between co-hosts **Mexico** and **South Africa**. Mexico won **2–0**

fact_verdicts.fact: Three red cards were shown (two South Africa, one Mexico) — a record for a World Cup opener
fact_verdicts.evidence: The match set a **World Cup record for the most red cards in an opening match**, with **three red cards** shown

fact_verdicts.fact: It was Mexico's first ever win in a World Cup opening match (previously 5 losses, 2 draws)
fact_verdicts.evidence: This was Mexico's **eighth time playing in a World Cup opener** and their **first win in an opener**, having previously lost five and drawn two

rationale: The answer does not mention the Iconic Tower as CTBUH's listed tallest completion for 2024 (fact 1 missing). It does mention the International Land-Sea Center forecast (fact 2 present). It discusses why the Chongqing tower was not completed in 2024, which addresses the disagreement (fact 3 present). However, the must-contain criterion requires both candidates (Iconic Tower and International Land-Sea Center) and the discrepancy to be present in the answer. The answer substitutes Greenland Shandong IFC for the Iconic Tower as the CTBUH pick, which contradicts fact 1.

fact_verdicts.fact: CTBUH's data lists Iconic Tower, Cairo (393.8 m, Africa's first supertall) as 2024's tallest completion
2024 forecast articles projected the International Land-Sea Center, Chongqing (458.2 m) as the year's tallest
fact_verdicts.evidence: Many pre-2024 forecasts and media articles, including from *The Real Deal* and *The B1M*, predicted that the **International Land-Sea Center** in Chongqing, China, would be the tallest building completed in 2024, at **458.2 m (1,503 ft)** with 98 floors.

fact_verdicts.fact: The disagreement hinges on whether the Chongqing tower's completion is dated 2024
fact_verdicts.evidence: The disagreement stems from three main factors: 1. **Forecasts vs. Actual Completions**: ... The International Land-Sea Center was expected to finish in 2024 but experienced delays.

failure.rationale: The trajectory shows the agent searched for "CTBUH 2024 tallest building completed Greenland Shandong International Financial Center" and similar queries, repeatedly landing on (and failing to fetch or properly parse) the CTBUH Data Handbook 2024 PDF. It never successfully fetched or extracted the Iconic Tower as the CTBUH-listed tallest completion. The searches were skewed toward the Greenland Shandong IFC (which originated from a speculative Reddit/social media post) rather than the actual CTBUH data for the Iconic Tower. This is a case where the right sources (CTBUH data) surfaced but the agent fetched/relied on wrong or low-quality pages (a Facebook post, a Reddit post, a partial PDF it couldn't parse) and missed the correct answer.

rationale: All three ground-truth facts are present in the answer: the 128 new moons bringing the total to 274 in March 2025, the discovery method (CFHT stacking, irregular retrograde moons), and the ongoing increase to 285 (and higher counts) depending on recognition date.

fact_verdicts.fact: The IAU recognized 128 new Saturnian moons on March 11, 2025, bringing the total to 274 at that time
fact_verdicts.evidence: As of **March 16, 2026**, Saturn has **285 confirmed moons**. On that date ... the IAU ... announced the confirmation of 11 additional natural satellites ... raising the total from 274 to 285. The 285 figure is the latest in a series of explosive discoveries. ... March 2025 — 128 new moons confirmed — 274

fact_verdicts.fact: The new moons were found by stacking Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope images; most are retrograde, 1-20 km
fact_verdicts.evidence: They were discovered by an international team led by Dr. Edward Ashton ..., using the 3.5‑meter Canada–France–Hawaii Telescope ... The vast majority of Saturn's moons are 'irregular' satellites ...

fact_verdicts.fact: Counts published since are higher still (285 or more during 2026) because newly recognized moons keep being added — the number depends on the recognition date
fact_verdicts.evidence: As of **March 16, 2026**, Saturn has **285 confirmed moons**. ... raising the total from 274 to 285. ... Counts published since are higher still (285 or more during 2026) ... because the confirmation process takes years ... there is therefore a large backlog ...

rationale: The answer includes UN-based estimates near 240 million and notes the 2006 census, and it mentions the skeptical 170-180 million figure. However, it fails to state that the National Population Commission has publicly admitted it lacks accurate population figures (fact 2 is missing), making the verdict partial.

fact_verdicts.fact: UN-based estimates are ~240 million (2026); the last census was 2006
fact_verdicts.evidence: The UN-based figures published by Worldometer place Nigeria as the 6th most populous country in the world and the most populous in Africa [Worldometer]. ... The last official headcount was in 2006.

fact_verdicts.fact: The National Population Commission has publicly admitted it lacks accurate population figures
Skeptics estimate the real figure could be 170-180 million; a biometric census has been planned
fact_verdicts.evidence: Some analysts (the “missing 50 million” thesis) 170–180 million ... a biometric census has been planned

failure.rationale: The agent's search queries focused on the general controversy, census politicization, and inflated figures, but never targeted the specific fact that the National Population Commission has publicly admitted it lacks accurate population figures. No query like "National Population Commission admits lacks accurate population figures" was issued, so the right source never surfaced.
